title: Interplay between Quantum Metric and Hybridized Collective Modes in Flat-Band Superfluids
authors: Yi Liu, Mingyan Wang, Penghui Hu, Yao Lu
abstract: We investigate collective excitations in flat-band superfluids by incorporating the coupled dynamics of pairing (phase and amplitude) and density fluctuations. We demonstrate that for any time-reversal symmetric superfluid system with an isolated flat band, only a single low-energy collective mode emerges in the long-wavelength limit. In contrast to the linearly dispersive Goldstone mode in conventional superfluids, this hybridized mode is gapless at zero momentum but exhibits a quadratic dispersion ($ω\propto q^2$) at small momenta. Analytically, we reveal that the dispersion coefficient of this collective mode is governed by the normal-state quantum metric of the flat band. These analytical predictions are in excellent agreement with numerical calculations. Our results are universally applicable to any generic $s$-wave flat-band superfluid, provided the flat band is energetically well separated from other dispersive bands.
summary: This theoretical work investigates collective excitations in flat-band superfluids, showing that the mode dispersion is not linear (like conventional superfluids) but quadratic ($\omega \propto q^2$). The key finding is that the curvature of this parabolic dispersion is universally determined by the normal-state quantum metric of the underlying flat band.
main_problem: To determine the low-energy collective excitation dispersion relation in flat-band superfluids by analyzing the coupled dynamics of pairing, amplitude, and density fluctuations.
main_result: The collective mode exhibits a quadratic dispersion ($\omega \propto q^2$) at small momenta, and the coefficient governing this curvature is determined by the normal-state quantum metric of the flat band.
method: The authors employ a path integral formalism with a Generalized Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation, analyzing the full dynamic response matrix via a multi-channel fluctuation expansion.
model_or_system: The system is a time-reversal symmetric superfluid characterized by an isolated flat band, modeled using a mean-field Hamiltonian projected onto the flat-band subspace.
key_observables: Collective excitation dispersion relation $\omega(q)$, the quadratic curvature coefficient $\kappa$, and the quantum metric $g_{qq}(k)$.
important_parameters: Zero temperature ($T=0$), long-wavelength limit ($q 	o 0$), and the existence of an isolated flat band.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the flat band is energetically well separated from other bands, and that the low-energy dynamics can be captured by projecting onto the degenerate null space of the static response matrix.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic band structure, the parabolic nature of the low-energy dispersion ($\omega \propto q^2$), and a direct comparison showing the fitted curvature tracking the theoretical quantum metric.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the coupled fluctuation dynamics (GHS transformation) to constructing the full dynamic response matrix, solving for the zero-frequency modes, and finally calculating the second-order spatial derivative of the matrix to extract the curvature coefficient.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous, geometric understanding of superfluid dynamics in highly constrained systems (flat bands), linking macroscopic excitation properties directly to fundamental quantum geometric invariants (the quantum metric).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic illustration of the normal-state band structure for a prototypical isolated flat-band system.

FIG. 2: Schematic of the 6 × 6 Bogoliubov–de Gennes (BdG) quasiparticle excitation spectrum for the Lieb lattice with a finite chemical potential and a staggered hopping amplitude fixed at δ = 0.3J. The spectrum features a pair of isolated perfectly flat bands separated by a distinct energy gap. (All energies are in units of J, and a = 1.)

FIG. 4: Scaling analysis of the collective mode dispersion along the x-direction (q = qˆx). (a) The extracted low-energy dispersion curves ω(q) for various staggered hopping parameters δ. (b) Log-log plot of the dispersion data. The parallel linear fits yield power-law exponents of α ≈2.0, confirming the parabolic nature of the excitations and the absence of a linear term. (The frequency ω is plotted in units of J.)

FIG. 3: The low-energy collective mode dispersion ω(qx, qy), obtained by numerically solving det[M(q, ω)] = 0 for small momenta at a fixed staggered hopping δ = 0.3J. The gapless excitation spectrum forms a parabolic surface. (Energies are in units of J, with a = 1.)

FIG. 5: Collective mode dispersion curvature κ (evaluated along the qx direction) versus the average normal-state quantum metric under varying staggered hoppings δ. The circular markers represent the data obtained directly from the multi-channel response matrix, while the solid line represents the analytical prediction κ = 2E
